content:
# Matthew Chapter 28 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_028_page_001.jpg, MAT_028_page_002.jpg

1. Now after the Sabbath, toward the dawn of the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary went to see the tomb.

2. And behold, there was a great earthquake, for an angel of the Lord descended from heaven and came and rolled back the stone and sat on it.

3. His appearance was like lightning, and his clothing white as snow.

4. And for fear of him the guards trembled and became like dead men.

5. But the angel said to the women, "Do not be afraid, for I know that you seek Jesus who was crucified.

6. He is not here, for he has been raised, as he said. Come, see the place where he lay.

7. Then go quickly and tell his disciples that he has been raised from the dead, and behold, he is going before you to Galilee; there you will see him. See, I have told you."

8. So they departed quickly from the tomb with fear and great joy, and ran to tell his disciples.

9. And behold, Jesus met them and said, "Greetings!" And they came up and took hold of his feet and worshiped him.

10. Then Jesus said to them, "Do not be afraid; go and tell my brothers to go to Galilee, and there they will see me."

11. While they were going, behold, some of the guard went into the city and told the chief priests all that had taken place.

12. And when they had assembled with the elders and taken counsel, they gave a sufficient sum of money to the soldiers

13. and said, "Tell people, 'His disciples came by night and stole him away while we were asleep.'

14. And if this comes to the governor's ears, we will satisfy him and keep you out of trouble."

15. So they took the money and did as they were directed. And this story has been spread among the Jews to this day.

16. Now the eleven disciples went to Galilee, to the mountain to which Jesus had directed them.

17. And when they saw him they worshiped him, but some doubted.

18. And Jesus came and said to them, "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.

19. Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit,

20. teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you. And behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

The final chapter. The resurrection and the Great Commission. All three translators handle this climactic text with precision and reverence. Full consensus on all major renderings.

**Verse 6 — "has been raised" (passive):** All three translators correctly preserve the passive voice ἠγέρθη. EMET and LOGOS both note the theological significance: God is the agent of resurrection. SOPHIA affirms this is non-negotiable — any rendering that shifts to active voice ("he rose") obscures the NT's consistent theological claim.

**Verse 9 — χαίρετε:** EMET renders "Greetings!" LOGOS likewise. PNEUMA renders "Greetings!" but notes the literal meaning "Rejoice!" SOPHIA follows all three with "Greetings!" — the everyday quality of the word is part of its power. PNEUMA's note about the ironic parallel with Judas's χαῖρε in 26:49 (noted by EMET) is a brilliant observation.

**Verse 17 — "but some doubted":** All three translators preserve this startling admission. PNEUMA and LOGOS both note the extraordinary honesty of this detail. SOPHIA affirms: Matthew's refusal to sanitize the disciples' response is one of the hallmarks of authentic testimony. The Great Commission is given to a community that includes doubt — not despite the doubt, but through it.

**Verses 19-20 — The Great Commission:** All three translators render this identically in substance. EMET's note on the grammatical structure (one main imperative μαθητεύσατε with three modifying participles) is important for understanding the passage's architecture. PNEUMA's observation about the fourfold "all" (authority, nations, things, days) and LOGOS's note about the inclusio with Emmanuel (1:23) are complementary insights that together illuminate the Gospel's grand design.

**The trinitarian formula (v.19):** All three translators note the singular "name" (ὄνομα) governing three persons. SOPHIA affirms: this is the text as written in Sinaiticus, and it stands as the most explicit trinitarian statement in the Gospels.

**Final note — The Gospel of Matthew:** With this chapter, the translation of Matthew from Codex Sinaiticus is complete. Matthew's Gospel begins with a genealogy linking Jesus to Abraham and David, and ends with a commission to "all nations." It begins with "God with us" (1:23) and ends with "I am with you always" (28:20). The arc is from Israel to the world, from promise to presence. The four translators — EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA, and SOPHIA — have rendered all 28 chapters, 1,071 verses, faithfully from the oldest substantially complete manuscript of the New Testament.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
